blurb: TTLs, eviction, stampedes, and write strategies.

questions.q: A cached value expires and 10,000 concurrent readers all miss at once, pounding the origin. What's this called?
questions.explanation: Cache stampede (a.k.a. thundering herd). Mitigations: per-key request coalescing (one fetch in flight), TTL jitter, XFetch probabilistic early recomputation.

questions.q: Which eviction policy is best for workloads with strong temporal locality (recently-used items likely to be re-used)?
questions.explanation: LRU assumes recency predicts future access. LFU is better when popularity is more stable than recency. Random is surprisingly close to LRU on many workloads.

questions.q: Write-through vs write-back vs write-around. Which gives the fastest write path AND lowest read miss rate for recently-written data?
questions.explanation: Write-back: writes go to cache first (fast) and flush to DB async (still cached). Write-through is durable but slow. Write-around never caches on write, so reads of just-written data miss.

questions.q: You're caching user profiles at the application tier. A user updates their name. Which approach most reliably keeps reads consistent?
choices: Cache-aside with explicit invalidate on write
questions.explanation: Cache-aside: app reads cache-or-DB; on write, app updates DB and explicitly invalidates the cache key. Write-through also works but couples the layers.

questions.q: Why does TTL jitter (±10%) help against cache stampedes?
choices: Prevents a popular batch of keys from expiring simultaneously
questions.explanation: If 1000 keys are all cached at T=0 with TTL=60s, they all expire at T=60 → 1000 concurrent stampedes. Jitter spreads expiries across a window.

questions.q: A CDN has tiered cache (edge → regional → origin). Why is this layering valuable?
choices: Regional tier absorbs edge misses, drastically reducing origin load
questions.explanation: Edge caches can only hold a subset of content. A regional (mid-tier) cache sees aggregated misses from many edges, hits at much higher rate than any individual edge — cuts origin load ~10×.

questions.q: Redis cluster has a hot key receiving 500K GETs/sec. One shard can't keep up. What's the standard mitigation?
choices: Key replication across multiple shards + client-side load balancing
questions.explanation: Replicate the hot key across N shards (salt the key with a hash suffix); clients read-load-balance across the replicas. Common at Twitter, Discord for celebrity-key hotspots.

questions.q: What's the Vary header's role in HTTP caching?
choices: Declares which request headers affect the response — cache must key by them
questions.explanation: Vary: Accept-Encoding means the same URL has different cached versions based on the client's encoding support. Skipping Vary causes serving gzipped content to a client that didn't request it.

questions.q: Your e-commerce site runs a flash sale at noon. At 12:00:01 the product-detail cache TTL expires and your DB connection pool saturates within 3 seconds. Requests start timing out. What mitigation would have prevented this?
choices: Request coalescing (singleflight) so only one cache miss triggers a DB fetch while others wait
questions.explanation: Request coalescing (Go's singleflight, nginx proxy_cache_lock) ensures only one request per key goes to the origin on a miss. All concurrent readers wait for that single fetch result. This prevents the stampede from overwhelming the DB.

questions.q: After deploying a new app version, you notice cache hit ratio dropped from 95% to 20% even though nothing changed in the cache layer. What's the most likely cause?
choices: The new code changed the cache key format, making all old entries unreachable
questions.explanation: A common post-deploy issue: if cache keys include a version prefix, serialization format change, or different parameter ordering, every key becomes a new key. The old cached data is still there but unreachable. Always ensure cache key compatibility or pre-warm after key format changes.

questions.q: Your Redis instance is at 95% memory. LRU eviction kicks in and starts evicting session tokens. Users are getting logged out randomly. How do you fix this without adding more RAM immediately?
choices: Separate session data into its own Redis instance with noeviction policy; use LRU only for the cache instance
Reduce TTL on all keys to 10 seconds
questions.explanation: Mixing cache data (evictable) with session/state data (must not be evicted) in the same Redis instance is a classic mistake. Separate them: cache instance uses allkeys-lru, session instance uses noeviction and alerts when full.

questions.q: A microservice queries a downstream API that returns user preferences. You cache the result for 5 minutes. During a 10-minute downstream outage, what happens to users whose cache entries expire mid-outage?
choices: They get errors because the cache miss triggers a failed downstream call
Nothing — cache never expires during outages
questions.explanation: Standard cache-aside will try to refresh from the downstream on miss and fail. The fix is 'stale-while-revalidate' or 'stale-on-error': serve the expired cached value when the origin is unreachable. Many HTTP caches and libraries support this pattern.

questions.q: You're running Memcached with consistent hashing across 5 nodes. One node dies. What happens to the keys that were on that node?
choices: All keys across all nodes are lost
Only keys on the dead node are lost; other nodes' keys are unaffected, but the dead node's keys cause cache misses until re-populated
Keys automatically migrate to other nodes
Memcached replicates, so nothing is lost
questions.explanation: Memcached has no replication. Consistent hashing means only ~1/N of keys (those on the dead node) become misses. Without consistent hashing (modulo), ALL keys would remap. The misses cause a temporary load spike on the origin until the keys are re-cached on the new ring assignment.
